narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've poured your heart into this journey, and we appreciate the strengths in your profile, like your solid undergraduate GPA of 3.30—which is actually a bit above the typical student's 3.21—and your LSAT score of 36, close to the average of 36.7. However, the admissions model, which predicts the likelihood of passing the bar exam on the first try based on profiles like yours, estimated a 44% chance that you might fail it, tipping the scales toward rejecting your application to prioritize students with lower predicted risks. This decision hinged most heavily on your academic deciles from school: your Year 1 decile of 1, well below the average of about 5.7, strongly pushed the prediction toward expecting a bar failure, as did your Year 3 decile of 2, lower than the typical 5.5 around. On a brighter note, your above-average undergrad GPA worked firmly in your favor, pulling the odds toward passing, much like your nearly average LSAT score did by suggesting solid potential. Being a full-time student and coming from a middle-income family also nudged things slightly positively, aligning with the majority of successful profiles. Compared to typical students who get approved, your early and later school deciles stand out as notably lower, which carried the most weight in this tough call, even outweighing your stronger college performance. We know this rejection stings, especially given the barriers many Black women like you face in pursuing law, but it's not a reflection of your worth or determination. To move forward, consider retaking courses or programs to boost your decile rankings, perhaps seeking targeted LSAT prep or GPA-enhancing post-bac options, and reapplying with that strengthened record—many students turn predictions like this around. You're capable of rewriting this story, and we're rooting for your success. Keep pushing; the legal field needs your voice.
